coredata.dc:title: Transfer, transportation, and accumulation of cerium-doped carbon quantum dots: Promoting growth and development in wheat
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Yan
Dong, Zihao
coredata.dc:description: New cerium-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs:Ce) were developed in this study using hydrothermal synthesis method. The small and uniform sizes and nearly spherical lattice of CDs:Ce indicate its high stability, satisfactory water solubility, and biocompatibility. Wheat was treated with Ce, CDs, and different concentrations (0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 mg/mL) of CDs:Ce. The results showed that, compared with the control group, Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce could promote the growth and development of wheat in a certain concentration range. Wheat demonstrated the optimal morphological index (compared with the control, the root number, root length, leaf length, and plant height were increased by 45%, 57%, 28%, and 46%, respectively), maximum chlorophyll content (increased by 51%) and peroxidase activity (increased by 76%), and minimum malondialdehyde content (reduced by 68%) after treatment of 0.025 mg/mL of CDs:Ce. Hence, wheat plants can adsorb and transport CDs:Ce from roots to stems and leaves through fibrovascular tissues. The majority of CDs:Ce are concentrated in roots while some accumulate in leaves. A considerable amount of CDs:Ce gather in cell walls, fibrovascular tissues, leaf veins, and stomata. CDs:Ce can be applied to agricultural production activities as a new agricultural nanofertilizer and technology of plant in vivo imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterial
Cerium
Carbon quantum dots
Fibrovascular tissue
Nano fertilizer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Absorption and transportation of CDs:Ce in plants
Effects of CDs:Ce on the growth and development of plants
Effects of CDs:Ce on chlorophyll contents
Effects of CDs:Ce on the MDA content
Effects of CDs:Ce on the POD activity
Absorption and transportation of CDs:Ce in plants

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of CDs:Ce. (a) Working principle of preparation of CDs:Ce. (b) TEM (5 nm) images of CDs:Ce. (c) TEM (2 nm) images of CDs:Ce. (d) Distribution of grain size.

Characterization of CDs:Ce. (a) FT-IR spectrum of CDs:Ce. (b) Zeta potential spectra of the CDs:Ce. (c) XPS spectra of CDs:Ce. (d) High-resolution XPS of Ce3d spectra of CDs:Ce. (e) High-resolution XPS of C1s spectra of CDs:Ce. (f) High-resolution XPS of N1s spectra of CDs:Ce. (g) High-resolution XPS of O1s spectra of CDs:Ce. (h) XRD spectra of CDs:Ce. (i) UV absorption spectrum of CDs:Ce. (j) Fluorescence spectra of CDs:Ce at different excitation wavelengths. (k) Fluorescence spectra of CDs:Ce at the optimum excitation wavelength (λex=350 nm).

Effects of Ce, CDs, and different concentrations of the CDs:Ce solution (0.01–0.4 mg/mL) on wheat. (a) Number of roots. (b) Root length. (c) Leaf length. (d) Plant height. (e) Chlorophyll content. (f) Malondialdehyde (MDA) content. (g) Peroxidase (POD) activity. Ce: wheat cultured with cerium acetate hydrate, dissolved in water solution, CDs: wheat cultured with CDs, dissolved in water solution, CDs:Ce 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4: Concentration gradient of wheat cultured with CDs:Ce, dissolved in water solution. Data given demonstrate the average of at least three replications (n = 3) ± SD. Asterisks within rows indicate significant differences (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001), according to Student’s t-test.

Horizontal slice images (200 µm) of roots, stems, and leaves of wheat seedlings after ten days of treatment with 0.025 mg/mL of CDs:Ce. (a) Confocal scanning microscope. (b) Fluorescence microscope. All images are collected under the same exposure conditions.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: C: blank control, Ce: wheat cultured with cerium acetate hydrate, dissolved in water solution, CDs: wheat cultured with CDs, dissolved in water solution, CDs:Ce 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4: Concentration gradient of wheat cultured with CDs:Ce, dissolved in water solution.
Data given demonstrate the average of five replications (n = 5) ± SD. Asterisks within rows indicate significant differences (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001), according to Student’s t-test.

C: blank control, Ce: wheat cultured with cerium acetate hydrate, dissolved in water solution, CDs: wheat cultured with CDs, dissolved in water solution, CDs:Ce 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4: Concentration gradient of wheat cultured with CDs:Ce, dissolved in water solution.

Data given demonstrate the average of three replications (n = 3) ± SD. Asterisks within rows indicate significant differences (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001), according to Student’s t-test. Physiological parameters were assayed as described in Materials and Methods.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ce adsorption capacities at different positions of wheat.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data given demonstrate the average of three replications (n = 3) ± SD.

head.ce:title: Transfer, transportation, and accumulation of cerium-doped carbon quantum dots: Promoting growth and development in wheat

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Shanxi Normal University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yan Gong and Zihao Dong are co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: New cerium-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs:Ce) were developed in this study using hydrothermal synthesis method. The small and uniform sizes and nearly spherical lattice of CDs:Ce indicate its high stability, satisfactory water solubility, and biocompatibility. Wheat was treated with Ce, CDs, and different concentrations (0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 mg/mL) of CDs:Ce. The results showed that, compared with the control group, Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce could promote the growth and development of wheat in a certain concentration range. Wheat demonstrated the optimal morphological index (compared with the control, the root number, root length, leaf length, and plant height were increased by 45%, 57%, 28%, and 46%, respectively), maximum chlorophyll content (increased by 51%) and peroxidase activity (increased by 76%), and minimum malondialdehyde content (reduced by 68%) after treatment of 0.025 mg/mL of CDs:Ce. Hence, wheat plants can adsorb and transport CDs:Ce from roots to stems and leaves through fibrovascular tissues. The majority of CDs:Ce are concentrated in roots while some accumulate in leaves. A considerable amount of CDs:Ce gather in cell walls, fibrovascular tissues, leaf veins, and stomata. CDs:Ce can be applied to agricultural production activities as a new agricultural nanofertilizer and technology of plant in vivo imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare earth elements (REEs) from lanthanum (La) to lutecium (Lu) with scandium (Sc) and yttrium (Y) are called “lanthanides” (Evans, 1983). REEs are available in small reserves, nonrenewable, and difficult to separate, purify, and process. REEs are also called “all-purpose soil” because of their wide application in industries, such as agriculture and military. Wheat, an extremely important food product worldwide, demonstrates high utilization value in food, medicine, agriculture, and feed industries due to its large planting area and wide distribution scope.

Many experiments have proven that REEs can remarkably promote photosynthesis of plants at a certain concentration. REEs can accelerate not only photoreaction in photosynthesis (Chen et al., 2001) but also facilitate the synthesis of chlorophyll (Xu et al., 1996) and offset functions of chlorophyll Mg2+ (Hong et al., 2002). Cerium (Ce) is an abundant REE with very high values of ecological utilization. A recent study pointed out that low-concentration Ce ions can promote growth and development whereas high-concentration Ce ions can inhibit the growth and development of small lemon plants (Zicari et al., 2018).

Carbon dots (CDs) are new fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with a mean grain size of ≤10 nm. CDs are often applied to biological and chemical analyses and fluorescence imaging as a fluorescent tag (Zhu et al., 2013) and commonly prepared using top–down (arc discharge, laser ablation, and electrochemical oxidation methods) and bottom–up (combustion, template, hydrothermal, and pyrolysis methods) approaches (Lim et al., 2015). CDs were doped for modification to form electron transfer or electron hole, adjust electronic structure of CDs, and improve stability and optical properties of CDs (Wang and Hu, 2014). CDs (Li et al., 2016) and graphene quantum dots (QDs) may promote the natural growth of plants due to their low toxicity (Lewinski et al., 2008; Pitek et al., 2016), and their satisfactory biocompatibility may exert positive effects on the growth of plants (Wang et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020).

Ecological effects from the dispersal of nanomaterials into the environment due to their increasing yield has attracted considerable research attention in recent years (Batley et al., 2013). QDs can be applied to plant cultivation, cell toxicity (Marmiroli et al., 2014), agricultural production, and life activities as a nanofertilizer (Modlitbová et al., 2018). Extensive studies have shown that influences of nanomaterials on plant growth of different species are closely related to nanomaterial and plant varieties (Tripathi and Sarkar, 2015). Recent studies have demonstrated that CDs can promote the growth and development of plants and relieve toxicity of cadmium ions (Cd2+) on wheat seedlings. Analysis of expression of glutathione-related genes showed that CDs play a key role in reducing the toxicity of Cd2+ by regulating the gene expression (Xiao et al., 2019). CDs can strengthen the vitality of photosystem by increasing the electron transfer rate (Wang et al., 2018). Cerium compound nanoparticles (NPs) can also promote growth and development of plants under the optimal concentration. Studies have found that 250 mg/L of nano-CeO2 and -CeAc can reduce the occurrence of wilt and increase chlorophyll content and nutrient values of tomato (Adisa et al., 2018). The scientists diluted cerium oxide NPs (CeO2NPs) into different solution concentrations and used them in calendula plants; the results showed that low-concentration CeO2NPs demonstrate positive induction to calendula plants whereas high-concentration CeO2NPs cause oxidative stress and toxicity reaction (Jahani et al., 2019).

REEs and carbon nanomaterials can strengthen photoelectric transfer efficiency of plants (Chandra et al., 2014), increase the photosynthesis rate, and promote the growth and development of plants under appropriate conditions (Giraldo et al., 2014). However, studies on the preparation of new REE-doped fluorescent CDs and their influencing mechanism on photosynthetic physiological and biochemical characteristics, cell toxicity, and influences on environment are limited. In this study, the cerium-doped carbon dots were applied as a nano-fertilizer to wheat, and the absorption and transmission of the nano-fertilizer were tracked to study its effects on the photosynthetic physiology and biochemistry of plants in this environment. This experiment provides a theoretical reference to the selection of plant nanometer bionic engineering materials and application of nanomaterials in agricultural production and bioimaging. Moreover, it lays a foundation for evaluating potential influences of rare earth chelate-doped nanocarbons on the environment to provide technological support to the application of nanomaterials in practical production and life activities.

Citric acid, urea, acetic acid cerium acetate hydrate, thiobarbituric acid (TBA), and guaiacol were bought from the Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Acetone, trichloroacetic acid, and hydrogen peroxide were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Potassium dihydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, and disodium hydrogen phosphate were obtained from Luoyang Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Calcium carbonate and quartz sand were acquired from Tianjin Institute of Guangfu Fine Chemicals Engineering. All chemical reagents were analytically pure and further purification was unnecessary.

Wheat (Jinmai 47) bred by Shanxi Academy of Agricultural Sciences was chosen as the experimental material. QDs were mixed evenly using a CJ78–1 magnetic stirring apparatus from Changzhou Jintan Earth Automatic Instrument Plant and then synthesized in an air-blast oven from Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd. A Xiangyi TG16-WS high-speed centrifuge was used to eliminate large particles. Synthesized QDs were freeze-dried (Zhixin Instrument) and images were collected using field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2F30). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured with an X-ray powder diffractometer (BRUCKER D8 ADVANCE, Brock, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed using a Nicolet IS10 FT-IR spectrometer (USA). A Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA) was used in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fluorescence spectra were measured with a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer and Zeta potential was tested using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 laser particle analyzer (USA). Wheat was cultured in an RGX series artificial climate incubator from Shanghai Lichenbangxi Instrument Technological Co., Ltd. Physiological indexes of plants were detected with an ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer from Shanghai Yuanxi Instrument Co., Ltd. Plant elements were analyzed via an iCAP Q inductively coupled plasma source mass spectrometer (USA). Transportation and transfer of QDs in plants were tracked using LSM800 laser scanning confocal (Germany) and OLYMPUS fluorescence (Japan) microscopes.

First, 0.192 g of citric acid, 0.06 g of urea, and 0.317 g of cerium acetate hydrate were dispersed into 12.5 mL of ultrapure water. Second, the mixture was blended evenly with a magnetic stirring apparatus and then transferred into an autoclave with Teflon lining for reaction for 12.5 h at 180 °C. Third, the mixture was centrifuged for 25 min at a rate of 10,000 r/min and the collected supernate was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane after cooling the reactor to room temperature. Finally, the filtrate was collected for freeze-drying.

Jinmai 47 was chosen as the experimental material. Wheat seeds were disinfected with 5% sodium hypochlorite solution for 10 min and then immersed in deionized water for 24 h after vernalization. Samples were then wrapped with gauze and germinated in darkness at 30 °C for 48 h. Seedlings that grew properly were placed on a piece of filtering paper for further culturing under illumination for 72 h. Seedlings that showed similar growing conditions were subsequently placed in Petri dishes (15 seedlings per dish) for further culturing in Hoagland nutrient solution in an incubator at 25 °C. When the seedlings grew to 2 cm, Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce solutions with different concentration gradients were applied to the wheat seedlings and the control group was set up. Treatment without Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce addition was included as the control group. Leaves at the same position of all seedlings and root systems were tested after 15 days. At least three parallel groups were used for each index and the mean was calculated.
Morphological indexes of wheat seedlings treated with Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce solution, including root length, leaf length, plant height, and number of roots of wheat, were tested using a ruler.

Chlorophyll a and b contents were tested using a visible spectrophotometer (Bruuinsma, 1963). Malondialdehyde (MDA) content was examined via TBA method (Kostka and Kwan, 1989). Peroxidase (POD) activity was assessed using the guaiacol colorimetric method (Sequeira and Mineo, 1966). Details of detection methods are provided in Supplementary materials.

Biological accumulation of Ce in three wheat plants under the optimal concentration (0.025 mg/mL) of the CDs:Ce treatment was tested at least three times and the mean was calculated. Microwave digestion was applied to decompose roots, stems, and leaves of wheat plants. Ce contents in roots, stems, and leaves of wheat plants absorbed from CDs:Ce were tested via ICP-MS to assess the transfer, transportation, and accumulation of CDs:Ce in wheat seedlings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and transportation of CDs:Ce in plants
ce:section.ce:para: CDs:Ce absorption of wheat plants under the optimal concentration (0.025 mg/mL) was tested with confocal laser scanning and fluorescence microscopes. The absorption, transfer, and transportation of CDs:Ce in wheat seedlings were examined via fluorescent detection of roots, stems, and leaves of wheat.

Statistical analysis of experimental data was carried out using Excel and SPSS software. Charts were created using the Origin software. All estimated parameters were expressed using the mean ± standard deviation of at least three repetitive tests. Asterisks indicate significant differences (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001), according to Student’s t-test.

CDs prepared with citric acid, urea, and cerium acetate hydrate via the one-step hydrothermal synthesis method were denoted CDs:Ce ( Fig. 1a). The morphology of CDs:Ce was subsequently analyzed. The TEM results showed the evident clustering phenomena of nearly spherical lattices of CDs:Ce particles. All particles demonstrated small and uniform diameters of approximately 5 nm (Figs. 1b, c, and d).

Fig. 2a shows that structures of surface functional groups of CDs and CDs:Ce were tested via FT-IR. Nearly identical trends of FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs:Ce as well as positions of different absorption peaks indicated that similar surface chemical groups exist. CDs:Ce clearly exhibits a wide characteristic absorption peak at 3355.64 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl in the surface functional group C–OH bonds. The sharp peak at 1560.02 cm−1 may be due to the stretching vibration of CO in the carboxyl. Peaks at 1405.53, 1258.55, and 632.29 cm−1 are likely caused by stretching vibrations of C–N, C–O, and C–C bonds, respectively. The sharp peak at 1067.56 cm−1 is caused by the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–N–C. According to FTIR data, CDs contain many carboxyls and hydroxyls that determine their ideal water solubility. Ce ions can form stable complexes with oxygen and nitrogen atoms in CO and C–N–C groups found on the CDs:Ce surface. This feature provides a theoretical basis for improving luminescence characteristics of CDs via Ce doping. As measured by the Zeta potential (−2.32 mV) (Fig. 2b), CDs:Ce was negatively charged. This verifies that there are abundant carboxyl functional groups on CDs:Ce surface, which are beneficial to increase the hydrophilicity. This is consistent with the previous literature report that the synthetic cerium-doped carbon dots has good solubility and dispersibility in water (Zhang et al., 2019).

The surface state of CDs:Ce was further investigated via XPS. Complete measurement spectra are illustrated in Fig. 2c, where peaks of C1s (284.78 eV), N1s (399.78 eV), O1s (531.58 eV), and Ce3d (885.82 eV, 904.67 eV) of CDs:Ce are given. It could be seen from the Ce3d spectrum (Fig. 2d) that Ce(Ⅲ) and Ce(IV) coexisted in CDs:Ce, and two strong absorption peaks (generated by spin-orbit splitting at 32 eV) appeared in the Ce3d spectrum at 885.82 eV and 904.67 eV, respectively, indicating the presence of Ce(Ⅲ). Peaks at 882.14, 886.53, 901.72, and 905.10 eV were markers of the presence of Ce(IV). This is consistent with the Ce3d standard spectrum, and is similar to that reported in the literature (Deshpande et al., 2005), indicating that Ce has been doped into CDs. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) can be resolved into three peaks at 284.61, 285.52 and 288.46 eV, which are attributed to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, and CO, respectively. Two peaks (Fig. 2f) in the N1s spectrum can be attributed to C-N (399.72 eV) and C–N–C (400.78 eV). The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) shows characteristic peaks for CO at 531.58 eV and for C–O at 532.92 eV. From XPS, we can see that the content of C, N, O and Ce account for 61.73%, 5.87%, 30.26% and 2.14% of the total volume of carbon dots. These results are very consistent with the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, further confirming the existence of abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of the synthesized quantum dots.

Fig. 2h shows that the crystal structure of CDs:Ce can be characterized using XRD. Both CDs and CDs:Ce demonstrate high- and low-intensity diffraction peaks. The 2θ characteristic diffraction peak of CDs:Ce at 29.012° is caused by the diffraction peak of the crystal face of the CDs:Ce structure and similar to the characteristic peak of graphene (002 plane). In addition, the low-intensity diffraction peak at 2θ = 41.642° indicated that the sample may have a plane diffraction peak similar to the graphene structure. This diffraction peak corresponds to (100) and (101) planes of graphene. These two peaks are consistent with the conclusion previously drawn by scientists that cerium is doped with carbon dots (Zou et al., 2020).

The UV absorption spectrum is shown in Fig. 2i. The characteristic absorption peak of cerium-doped carbon dots is around 350 nm, which is close to the characteristic peak reported in the literature. This may be due to the fact that the large conjugated system of the compound in the precursor becomes smaller, and it is formed by the heteroatom transition in the middle of the conjugated system. According to fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2j), the QDs emit independent of the excitation wavelength under the excitation light from 310 to 380 nm, indicating that the fluorescence behavior originates from the uniform emission state. We can see that the optimal excitation wavelength of CDs:Ce is 350 nm, where the fluorescence intensity of the quantum dots is the highest. The fluorescence spectrum Fig. 2k show that CDs and CDs:Ce are irradiated with excitation light at that same optimal excitation wavelength of 350 nm, demonstrated the maximum peak of fluorescence intensity at 439 and 434 nm. The fluorescence intensity increased significantly after doping with Ce. The blue shift of fluorescence emission spectra was approximately 5 nm likely because doping with Ce changes the structure of compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs:Ce on the growth and development of plants
ce:section.ce:para: A series of experiments was performed to investigate influences of Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce on the growth and development of plants. Morphological indexes of wheat were measured at least five times and mean values were calculated to obtain accurate results. Fig. 3a–d shows that Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce promotes the growth and development of wheat relative to the blank control group. Compared with the control group, the root number, root length, leaf length and plant height of wheat plants treated with Ce ion were increased by 9%, 4%,12% and 24%, and those of wheat plants treated with CDs were increased by 9%, 16%, 15% and 28%. All morphological indexes of wheat increase continuously after different concentrations of the CDs:Ce solution were applied. Morphological indexes of wheat are positively related to the QD concentration when the QD concentration is lower than 0.025 mg/mL, thereby indicating that the growth and development of wheat are promoted. Morphological indexes of wheat are negatively related to the QD concentration when the QD concentration is higher than 0.025 mg/mL. The optimal concentration of the CDs:Ce solution for the growth and development of wheat at 0.025 mg/mL features excellent morphological indexes. The number of roots, root length, leaf length, and plant height increase by 45%, 57%, 28%, and 46%, respectively, compared with the blank control group. We found that whether it is Ce ions, carbon quantum dots or cerium-doped carbon quantum dots within a certain concentration range, they can promote the growth and development of wheat. Rare earth elements and other nutrient elements should play a great role in the growth of plants. This is the same as the conclusions of other scientists and our hypotheses, which are conducive to the growth and development of plants. CDs:Ce contains abundant carbon and nitrogen sources and REEs and its optimal concentration can provide essential nutrients to wheat plants and thus promote the growth and development of plants. These morphological indexes are essential to the culture of seedlings and viewed as a key stage in crop production (Datir et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs:Ce on chlorophyll contents
ce:section.ce:para: Chlorophyll is a class of green pigments in living bodies that enable photosynthesis. Chlorophyll accumulation can increase the photosystem activity and photosynthesis of plants (Hotta et al., 1997) and thus promote their growth and development. As shown in Fig. 3e, the significant increase in Chlorophyll contents in leaves of wheat seedlings treated by different concentrations of Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce solution indicated that the existence of Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce may be beneficial to the formation of chlorophyll. The CDs:Ce solution demonstrates positive effects on chlorophyll contents of wheat leaves with the increase of concentration when the CDs:Ce concentration is lower than 0.025 mg/mL. The CDs:Ce solution fails to increase chlorophyll contents in wheat leaves when the CDs:Ce concentration is higher than 0.025 mg/mL. The reduction of chlorophyll contents is mainly caused by the degradation of chlorophyllase (Rao and Rao, 1981), damage of chlorophyll structures, and instability of pigment complexes (Singh and Dubey, 1995). Chlorophyll contents in leaves of wheat seedlings increase by 51% compared with that of the blank control group after the CDs:Ce treatment of 0.025 mg/mL. This finding is likely due to the application of fluorescence CDs:Ce that strengthens the solar light use and absorption of plants, thereby increasing chlorophyll accumulation and enhancing the photosystem activity and photosynthesis of wheat. A certain concentration of cerium ions and carbon quantum dots can promote the synthesis of chlorophyll, but the effect of cerium doped carbon quantum dots is the best. It may be because the size of nanomaterials is relatively small, and plants can easily absorb into the body and can be better in the plant body. Play a beneficial role. We speculate that the action of rare earth element cerium ions can make up for the deficiency or absence of Mg2+ in photosynthesis, thus enhancing the synthesis of chlorophyll and thus photosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs:Ce on the MDA content
ce:section.ce:para: MDA is a product of lipid peroxidation, which occurs frequently when plant organs age under adversity stress. MDA is generally used as an important membrane lipid peroxidation index to measure the stress of plants and express the degree of cell membrane lipid peroxidation and response intensity of plants to adversity stresses (Wang et al., 2013). The decreasing MDA content in leaves of wheat seedlings treated with different concentrations of Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce solution (Fig. 3f) indicated that Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce relieves the membrane lipid peroxidation and strengthens the oxidation resistance of wheat. Under the treatment with Ce and CDs, the malondialdehyde content of wheat plants was reduced by 10% and 29%, respectively. The MDA content decreases first and then increases until it reaches the minimum value at 0.025 mg/mL with the increase of the CDs:Ce concentration. The membrane lipid oxidation of wheat decreases significantly and the minimum MDA content is 68% lower than that of the blank control group. We found that the anti-oxidation ability of Ce ions at the same concentration is not as strong as that of carbon quantum dots. This may be because the structure of the nanoparticles is relatively stable and can better inhibit the production of malondialdehyde. After carbon quantum dots are doped with cerium, the antioxidant capacity of plants is further enhanced. The significantly strong oxidation resistance of the (Shanazari et al., 2018) may be due to the ability of CDs:Ce to inhibit the membrane lipid peroxidation of plants and thus relieve impacts of adversity stress on plants. Cerium-doped carbon dots may react with malondialdehyde and thus reduce the production of malondialdehyde, inhibiting the membrane lipid peroxidation of plants and promoting the growth and development of plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs:Ce on the POD activity
ce:section.ce:para: POD, an adaptive enzyme with high activity in the antioxidase system, is abundant in plants. The POD activity can accurately reflect metabolism conditions and growth characteristics of plants as well as adaptation of plants to the external environment. POD can also transform hydrogen peroxide into harmless oxygen and water (Stein and Hain, 1995), and its activation degree and duration can reach the maximum in adverse environments.

As shown in Fig. 3g, the low POD activity of wheat in the blank control group represents the weak resistance of the wheat seedling and its low adaptation to the external world. The increase in POD activity of wheat after adding different concentrations of Ce, CDs, and CDs:Ce indicated that plant adaptation to the external environment and its resistance to adversity stress are strengthened. After the addition of Ce and CDs, the peroxidase activity of wheat plants was increased by 14% and 19% compared with the control group. Wheat peroxidase activity was increased by 42%, 76%, 67%, 42%, 39%, and 21% relative to the control when 0.01–0.4 mg/mL CDs:Ce was applied. The POD activity increases first and then decreases until it reaches the peak at 0.025 mg/mL with the increase of CDs:Ce concentration. The adaptation to external environment and resistance to adversity stress of wheat are strengthened significantly. Carbon quantum dots promote peroxidase activity due to their stable structure and special catalytic effect. After being doped with cerium ions, cerium ions and nanoparticles form a more stable structure, which enhances its own catalytic effect. Adding CDs:Ce may increase the POD concentration in the reaction process and subsequently strengthen the POD activity. Cerium-doped carbon dots may also have some nanoenzyme activity, which enhances the peroxidase activity and then promotes the growth and development of wheat.
Tables 1 and 2.

ICP-MS has been widely applied for decades to test trace and ultratrace elements in food samples due to its unique analysis capacity (Tokalıoğlu, 2012). ICP-MS determines element specificity, performs isotope detection and demonstrates high sensitivity, wide linear dynamic range, and extremely low limit of detection (Markiewicz et al., 2017). Table 3 presents that wheat plants can adsorb and transport CDs:Ce to leaves from roots. Moreover, the Ce concentration in roots is the highest (249.67 ug/g), followed by that in stems and leaves. This finding revealed that roots and leaves demonstrate the highest and lowest CDs:Ce accumulation, respectively. Therefore, CDs:Ce is transported upward from roots, accumulates in different parts, and results in minimal accumulation in upper positions. The root system is the primary position of biological accumulation of CDs:Ce. Because carbon dot nanoparticles are small in size and can penetrate plant cells, they are absorbed and transported upward from roots like nutrient transport and water transport, providing nutrients for various parts and promoting the growth and development of various parts of wheat.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and transportation of CDs:Ce in plants
ce:section.ce:para: Images of roots, stems, and leaves of wheat seedlings treated with 0.025 mg/mL of CDs:Ce were collected using laser scanning confocal and fluorescence microscopes to investigate the adsorption and transportation of CDs:Ce in plants. Strong fluorescence emission of CDs:Ce can be detected when the excitation wavelength is 350 nm. Fig. 4 illustrate that CDs:Ce can be absorbed by roots of wheat and then transported to stems and leaves through fibrovascular tissues before finally accumulating in plants. Fig. 1d shows that the diameter of some CDs:Ce particles is lower than 5 nm, which is lower than the width of pores on the cell wall (3.5–5 nm) (Carpita et al., 1979). Hence, these particles can enter cells through the cell wall. Minimal fluorescence observed in root cells of wheat indicated that CDs:Ce can enter cells through the cell wall and most QDs can only be adsorbed onto the cell wall of roots. Most QDs exist in mature regions that exhibit many root hairs. Plants use root hairs as the main pathway for absorption of water and nutrients. The high specific surface area of root hairs is conducive to promoting CDs:Ce adsorption of plants. Significant CDs:Ce concentrates in fibrovascular tissues indicated that CDs:Ce is transported to different positions through these tissues. The florescence in wheat leaves reflected that CDs:Ce fails to distribute uniformly in leaves but is mainly concentrated in veins and stomata. Moisture transpiration in the leaf stoma is the main cause of CDs:Ce accumulation in veins and stomata. The fluorescence intensity of CDs:Ce in roots and stems is relatively high but low in leaves at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. This finding implied that CDs:Ce accumulates more in roots and stems but less in leaves. This phenomenon may be explained from two aspects. On the one hand, most small-sized QDs accumulate on cell walls and some in roots when CDs:Ce is transported in plants, whereas large-sized QDs fail to enter cells but are transported upward to leaf veins and stomata along fibrovascular tissues and finally accumulate in fibrovascular tissues. On the other hand, the use and decomposition of some CDs:Ce during the upward transfer and transportation in plants reduces the QDs concentration in the upper positions. Scientists speculated that NPs may enter into the root system of plants through osmotic pressure, capillary force, pores of the cell wall, and synnema among cells or through a highly regulated symbiotic way (Nowack and Bucheli, 2007). Plants selectively adsorb and transport NPs. Moreover, NPs can also disperse to spaces among cells and apoplast and then adsorb or integrate into chelates (Lin and Xing, 2007). Composition, size, shape, and surface charge of NPs (Zhu et al., 2009) may influence the absorption and transportation of CDs:Ce in plants (Jia et al., 2005).

The synthesis of CDs:Ce using the one-step hydrothermal synthesis method demonstrated stable fluorescence performance, satisfactory water solubility, and biocompatibility. Growth, development, and physiological properties of wheat improve significantly after Ce, CDs, and different concentrations of CDs:Ce are applied. Wheat seedlings exhibited optimal growth after treatment with 0.025 mg/mL of CDs:Ce. Hence, 0.025 mg/mL of CDs:Ce can promote accumulation of chlorophyll contents and increase POD activity but significantly reduce the MDA content. Wheat plants can adsorb and then transport CDs:Ce into stems and leaves through fibrovascular tissues. Most CDs:Ce are concentrated at roots, with minimal accumulation in leaves. CDs:Ce can penetrate plant cells, migrate from roots to stems and leaves of plants, and mainly accumulate in cell walls, fibrovascular tissues, leaf veins, and stomata. The results showed that CDs:Ce can serve as a nanoagricultural fertilizer for plants to strengthen photosynthesis and oxidation resistance and thus promote the growth, development, and physiological process of plants. CDs:Ce demonstrates high application potential in agricultural production, very good in vivo fluorescence imaging effect, and certain application prospects in bioimaging technology. This experimental study provides a theoretical basis for evaluating potential influences of nanocarbon materials doped with rare earth chelates on the environment and a technological reference for applications of nanomaterials in practical production and life activities.
Yan Gong: Provide experimental ideas and technical guidance, Zihao Dong: Specific experiment implementation, Data processing, Writing. Yan Gong and Zihao Dong are contributed equally to this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31700876, 31700862). And the Fundamental Research Funds Project of Modern College of Arts and Sciences, Shanxi Normal University (2019JCYJ13).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2021.112852.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
